title: Killing Sacred Cows: Overcoming the Financial Myths That Are Destroying Your Prosperity
author: Garrett B. Gunderson, Stephen Palmer

chapters.title: Financial myths Wall Street treats as untouchable
chapters.text:
Which belief makes you feel small or stuck? Maybe you think only certain people are creative, or that one soulmate exists for everyone. Myths like these shrink possibility. In finance they are called "sacred cows" — ideas, institutions, or figures shielded from scrutiny.

Money myths grew out of history: Depression-era hoarding, post-WWII loyalty to corporate jobs as the only safe path. Parents and friends pass them along through anecdote. Some industries profit from keeping people misinformed.

A lie remains a lie even when millions repeat it.

Most people discover the trap too late. Dismantling five damaging myths — killing the sacred cows — lets you rethink money choices. This summary ends with concrete steps you can take today.

Sacred cows survive because they feel prudent. Questioning them can sound reckless until you notice how many "safe" choices quietly cap upside — staying in the wrong job for benefits, hoarding cash while inflation erodes it, or avoiding investment because someone once lost money in a downturn.

chapters.title: Myth one: Someone else's gain means your loss
chapters.text:
The scarcity myth treats money, opportunity, and resources as a fixed pie. One person's slice leaves none for others — a zero-sum contest that breeds competition and greed.

Enslavement by illusion is comfortable; it is the liberation by truth that people fear. ~ David Hawkins

Scarcity thinking trains fear-based decisions. "Limited time" deals push you to act before thinking, making you easy prey for scams. You assume someone else will grab what you hesitate over.

The mindset spills beyond finance. You skip purchases you can afford, resent others' wealth, and feel jealousy when peers succeed. Creative projects die before they start: "Too many bloggers already," or "My book could never compete." How desperate would that make you feel? It drains energy before you begin.

What you rehearse in your mind, you amplify in your life.

Abundance is the alternative — enough opportunity for everyone. Benefits include:

• Replacing "I can't afford it" with "How can I afford it?"
• Cooperating instead of competing.
• Shifting focus from survival to contribution and enjoyment.
• Feeling content before the next purchase or achievement.
• Investing deliberately rather than reactively.
• Trading anxiety for calm trust.

Through an abundance lens, others' success becomes evidence that yours is possible too.

Abundance does not mean ignoring budgets. It means treating wealth as creatable through value, partnerships, and learning rather than as a shrinking resource you must grab before neighbors do.

chapters.title: Myth two: Chasing employer security keeps you safe
chapters.text:
Many equate security with paychecks, benefits, retirement plans, and government programs. Experts push high-salary jobs with bonuses, yet that path makes you a replaceable part. Break the part; the machine keeps running.

Real security is what you build for yourself and your family.

Would you let a neighbor manage your house because theirs looks sturdier? Then why outsource financial well-being to institutions or government? Control belongs with you.

Avoiding responsibility in one domain can collapse the whole structure — including progress toward your Soul Purpose, the work that energizes you and serves others. Payment may follow, but meaning comes from applying talent to passion, even when that requires uncomfortable tradeoffs.

Responsibility generates energy.

Nobody else can secure your happiness, career, or wealth. A well-paid job you hate delivers short-term relief, not sustained creativity or fulfillment. You remain productive, creative, and ambitious only when work aligns with potential.

Trade the illusion of security for freedom: income tied to passion, unconventional choices, collaboration over rivalry, and full use of your abilities. Ask:

• What would I do unpaid because I love it?
• What life do I want?
• How can I help others?
• Where can my value compound?
• How do I confront financial fear?

Answers clarify direction. The closing section provides an actionable checklist.

Soul Purpose thinking reframes career moves. Instead of climbing a ladder because it exists, you ask which rungs align with strengths that others will pay for because they genuinely help.

chapters.title: Myth three: Wealth corrupts by nature
chapters.text:
Picture a rich person. Many see a selfish executive in expensive clothes. Media reinforced that caricature for decades, yet plenty of wealthy people are generous and thoughtful.

The myth twists scripture. Paul warned against the love of money, not money itself — obsession with accumulation can drive harmful choices.

People invoke the myth to justify staying broke: they "refuse to sell their soul" yet obsess over money anyway. Financial stress traps attention on bills instead of relationships — including the relationship with yourself.

Money is neutral; it represents agreed value. Coffee costs a few dollars; a masterpiece costs millions. Both transactions aim at mutual satisfaction.

A hundred-dollar bill is not objectively "worth a hundred dollars" to everyone. A teen might see headphones; a homeless person might see a week of meals.

Money works like language — a tool that can build or destroy.

Treat money as a means, not a mission. Good and harm both happen with or without large balances. Power sits with the chooser. Money magnifies values — and can multiply your capacity to help.

Did you know? A January 2024 CNBC report noted America added roughly half a million new millionaires in 2023, bringing the total near 7.5 million.

Rejecting wealth to appear virtuous often backfires. Broke people still make moral compromises; they simply have fewer options when choosing which ones.

chapters.title: Myth four: Insurance is wasted money
chapters.text:
Attitudes toward insurance split three ways: self-insurers who skip coverage, believers who protect accumulated assets, and skeptics who treat premiums as necessary evil.

The savings argument asks whether skipping insurance really leaves more cash. Transferring risk to an insurer frees mental bandwidth. You stop rehearsing disaster scenarios and protect your primary asset — yourself.

Without insurance you need liquid reserves matching exposed assets. Own a million-dollar home and keep a million in the bank? An uninsured fire drains savings to rebuild. Pay two thousand yearly in premiums and a fire costs only that annual payment plus deductible.

The math is straightforward; psychology matters too. Illness, accidents, and property loss happen. Insurance replaces what you cannot quickly replace alone.

Policies have nuances — consult experts and model scenarios before signing.

Those who nickel-and-dime small expenses sometimes lose big.

Complaints about "unused" coverage miss the point. Unused insurance means good fortune. Insurers handle tragedy daily; buy solid coverage and hope you never file.

Self-insurance works only when reserves truly match worst-case loss. Many people underestimate tail risks — disability, liability claims, or long hospital stays — until one event erases years of careful saving.

chapters.title: Myth five: Saving pennies equals earning them
chapters.text:
This myth traps bargain hunters who fill homes with sale items they never wanted. Black Friday, summer clearances, and buy-two-get-one deals exploit price fixation over value.

A bargain is something you cannot use at a price you cannot resist. ~ Franklin P. Jones

Price matters, but value leads. Imagine shopping for shrimp, spotting half-off chicken, buying two despite disliking meat, then trashing freezer-burned poultry because you lacked time or appetite.

Price-first shopping leaves stores overloaded with clutter — clothes worn once, trinkets forgotten — while your brain celebrates "savings" of twenty or forty dollars.

Question every purchase; question twice during sales.

The myth also poisons enjoyment of full-price experiences. A deserved vacation becomes a mental invoice. You postpone joy you can afford because the deal was not steep enough. Decide what the purchase means to you, then what it means to your wallet.

Value-based spending frees attention for earning and creating. Hours spent chasing coupons rarely return more than hours spent improving skills that raise income.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Replace outdated money myths with deliberate action aligned to your values:

• Question advice: Where did the idea originate? Who profits? What real examples support or refute it? List three pros and three cons before accepting it.
• Shift from "why" to "how": Replace "Why can't I afford this?" with "How can I afford it?" Brainstorm three ways to increase income or deliver more value.
• Define Soul Purpose: List strengths, passions, and how they serve others. This week take one small step aligning money choices with that purpose.
• Commit to learning: Pick one book, podcast, or course this month on finance. Wealthy people keep studying; so should you.
• Audit spending: Track every expense for seven days. Sort into necessities, meaningful investments, and impulse buys. Adjust.
• Spend consciously: Before nonessential purchases, ask whether you need the item or only the discount.
• Seek wise company: Talk monthly with someone whose financial thinking challenges and elevates yours.

Prosperity follows daily choices that honor growth and purpose. Start small, stay consistent.

Read it till the end?
